title: Future Leader: Jono Bowles, Industry Product Manager – Healthcare & Director of Industry Solutions, Ottimate
description: Jono Bowles, Industry Product Manager – Healthcare & Director of Industry Solutions at Ottimate, has been named to the Future Leaders Class of 2026 by Senior Housing News. To become a Future Leader, an individual is nominated by their peers. The candidate must be a high-performing employee who is 40 years of age or younger, […] The post Future Leader: Jono Bowles, Industry Product Manager – Healthcare & Director of Industry Solutions, Ottimate appeared first on Senior Housing News .
content:
Jono Bowles, Industry Product Manager – Healthcare & Director of Industry Solutions at Ottimate, has been named to the Future Leaders Class of 2026 by Senior Housing News.
To become a Future Leader, an individual is nominated by their peers. The candidate must be a high-performing employee who is 40 years of age or younger, a passionate worker who knows how to put vision into action, and an advocate for older adults and the committed professionals who ensure their well-being.
Bowles sat down with Senior Housing News to share what drew him to the senior housing & senior living industry, the biggest leadership lessons he has learned, his thoughts on the future of senior housing & senior living, and much more. To learn more about the Future Leaders Awards program, visit https://futureleaders.wtwhmedia.com/.
SHN: What drew you to the senior housing & senior living industry?
Bowles: What initially drew me to senior living was the opportunity to apply my healthcare technology experience to an industry that has an incredibly meaningful mission. Earlier in my career, I worked with large hospitals and health systems (and loved that, too), but I have found a real sense of purpose in my current focus on senior living. It’s an industry that doesn’t always receive the attention it deserves, yet it plays such a vital role for millions of families.
What has kept me here, though, is the people. I’ve had the privilege of getting to know operators, finance leaders, and caregivers who work tirelessly to improve the lives of older adults. Their dedication is remarkable, and it’s motivating to build products that make even a small part of their day easier.
The work is personal for me as well. My mom has spent years in a long-term care setting, and before that, my grandparents also received long-term care. Experiencing senior living as a family member has given me a deep appreciation for the trust families place in these organizations and the compassion required to deliver exceptional care every day.
As a product manager, I believe technology must empower the people providing that care, not add complexity. If we can reduce administrative work and streamline financial operations behind the scenes, we’re helping organizations spend more of their time and resources where they matter most: their residents.
SHN: How would you describe your leadership style, and how has it changed over time?
Bowles: My leadership style is grounded in leading by example and staying close to the work. I’ve always believed credibility comes from understanding the details – not just setting direction, but being willing to engage with the hardest problems alongside the team.
In my current role as an Industry Product Manager, that also means connecting directly with our customers. A big part of my responsibility is deeply understanding senior living operators: both their back-office and financial workflow, and the challenges they face day-to-day. Staying close to that reality is what allows me to translate customer needs into product decisions that are useful in practice, not just in theory. For me, leadership in product is largely about reducing the distance between customer reality and product decisions.
One example of that was recognizing how often protected health information (PHI) shows up in back-office workflows, particularly on invoices. Working closely with senior living operators, we helped design and launch a PHI redaction capability that automatically identifies and removes PHI from invoices, helping streamline processing and reducing compliance risk.
Early in my career, I was fortunate to work with leaders who embodied servant leadership. Their focus on removing obstacles, listening deeply, and creating space for others to do their best work shaped how I lead today. At its core, I try to balance staying grounded in the realities of the customer with empowering teams to operate with autonomy and ownership.
SHN: What is the biggest leadership lesson you’ve learned while serving the senior housing & senior living industry?
Bowles: One of the biggest leadership lessons I’ve learned in senior living is that there is no single “starting point” when it comes to technology maturity. Operators vary widely in how they use systems, workflows, and automation, not because of resistance, but because of the different realities, constraints, and histories of each organization.
That has reinforced for me the importance of meeting customers where they are. As a product leader, it’s easy to assume the goal is to drive standardization as quickly as possible. But in practice, progress happens when you design solutions that respect existing workflows while still guiding teams toward better, more efficient ways of working over time.
In senior living especially, change management is just as important as the technology itself. These organizations are focused on delivering consistent, high-quality care, often with lean administrative teams. If a solution feels disruptive or forces too much change at once, even if it’s technically “better,” it can create friction that slows adoption and ultimately limits impact.
The most effective approach I’ve seen is to make incremental progress by building trust, delivering value quickly, and then expanding from there. When you do that well, technology adoption becomes a journey that ultimately supports better care for residents and less administrative burden for staff.
SHN: In one word, how would you describe the senior housing & senior living industry and why?
Bowles: Humanity.
At its core, senior living is about people caring for people: residents, families, caregivers, and operators all working within a deeply personal, high-trust environment.
Even as technology and operations evolve, the most important constant is the human element. Every process, system, and workflow ultimately traces back to someone trying to provide better care or make life a little easier for a resident or their family.
That’s what makes the industry so meaningful to work in, and also why I think it requires such thoughtful approaches to product and leadership. If you lose sight of the human side, you miss what actually makes the industry work.
SHN: What do you see as the biggest opportunities and challenges currently facing the senior housing & senior living industry?
Bowles: One of the biggest dynamics shaping senior living right now is the combination of growing demand and increasing operational complexity. The aging population in the United States is creating sustained demand for senior housing and care services over the coming years. That long-term growth outlook is driving both urgency and opportunity for operators to scale and evolve.
At the same time, the industry is navigating meaningful challenges, particularly around staffing. Many organizations are working through persistent shortages across both care and administrative roles, which adds pressure on already lean teams and makes operational consistency harder to maintain. In many communities, teams are doing more with less across both care and back-office functions. Rising costs and increasing financial and regulatory complexity only add to that strain.
We’re also seeing continued evolution in ownership and operating models, with more consolidation and larger multi-site organizations becoming more common. That shift can create opportunities for standardization and investment in better systems, but it also increases the complexity of integrating people, processes, and technology across organizations.
From my perspective in healthcare technology, one of the biggest opportunities lies in helping address these pressures through better systems and automation. If we can reduce the administrative burden on understaffed teams, especially in areas like finance and back-office operations, it allows caregivers and operators to focus more of their time on residents and care delivery, where it matters most.
SHN: If you had a crystal ball, what do you think will impact the senior housing & senior living industry over the next 5-10 years?
Bowles: Over the next 5–10 years, I think the senior living industry will be shaped by a few reinforcing forces that are already in motion today.
The first is sustained demand growth driven by an aging population. That will continue to put pressure on the system, not just in terms of capacity, but in the ability of operators to scale care delivery consistently across more communities and more complex resident needs.
The second is the ongoing workforce challenge. Staffing shortages across both clinical and administrative roles are likely to remain one of the most defining constraints for the industry. That will force organizations to rethink how they structure work, how they support employees, and where they can use technology to reduce unnecessary burden.
The third is the continued evolution of technology, particularly around automation and AI. I don’t think the biggest impact will come from any single “breakthrough” tool, but rather from the gradual removal of manual, fragmented processes that still exist across finance, operations, and care-adjacent workflows. The organizations that win will be the ones that successfully translate technology into real operational relief for their teams.
More broadly, I think we’ll see a shift toward more standardized, data-driven operations across the industry. Not at the expense of the human side of care, but in service of it, so that staff can spend less time on administrative complexity and more time focused on residents.
SHN: In your opinion, what qualities must all Future Leaders possess?
Bowles: In my opinion, Future Leaders in senior living need a combination of humility, curiosity, and a strong bias toward action.
Humility is important because this is an industry where the stakes are deeply human. Whether you’re working with caregivers, operators, or families, you’re operating in an environment where trust is everything. The best leaders recognize that they don’t have all the answers and stay close to the people doing the work every day.
Curiosity is equally critical. Senior living is complex, with a wide range of operating models, systems, and constraints. Leaders who take the time to understand those nuances and really listen to customers and frontline teams are the ones who build solutions that actually work in practice, not just in theory.
Finally, a bias toward action matters because the challenges in this industry are real and immediate. It’s not enough to have thoughtful ideas; leaders need to be able to translate insight into execution, iterate quickly, and continuously improve based on feedback from the field.
The leaders who stand out in this space are those who balance all three by staying grounded in the human side of care while still moving decisively to improve how the industry operates.
summary: Bowles sat down with Senior Housing News to share what drew him to the senior housing & senior living industry, the biggest leadership lessons he has learned, his thoughts on the future of senior housing & senior living, and much more. title: The future of LTC leadership: Collaborative, curious and courageous
description: Editor’s note: This is the fifth and final article in an ongoing series. Make sure to check out the previous articles Rebuilding public trust, one transparent step at a time, Evidence-based practice without the eye roll, Stronger together: How coalition leadership is transforming long-term care in Iowa and The workforce crisis isn’t just staffing. It’s culture. As this series…
content:
Editor’s note: This is the fifth and final article in an ongoing series. Make sure to check out the previous articles Rebuilding public trust, one transparent step at a time, Evidence-based practice without the eye roll, Stronger together: How coalition leadership is transforming long-term care in Iowa and The workforce crisis isn’t just staffing. It’s culture.
As this series comes together, one pattern stands out. Whether the focus has been evidence-based practice, workforce culture, coalition leadership or public trust, each conversation points in the same direction. Progress in long-term care becomes more achievable when it is shared.
The challenges facing leaders today are not new, but their intensity and visibility have grown. Leaders are expected to strengthen outcomes, support and retain staff, improve culture, and respond to increasing scrutiny, all at the same time. For many years, the expectation was that this work would be managed within a single organization. Leaders carried that responsibility largely on their own.
What we have seen across each of these discussions is that this model has limits. It slows improvement, increases pressure and makes it harder to sustain meaningful change over time.
A different approach is taking shape. Instead of working in parallel, leaders are starting to work in connection with one another. That shift is subtle, but it changes how problems are understood and how solutions develop. When organizations share what they are seeing and what they are trying, challenges become more manageable, and progress becomes more consistent.
Where collations create change
This progress was especially clear in the conversation about the workforce. Retention and stability are influenced less by staffing numbers alone and more by whether people feel supported and valued in their work. Leaders set that tone, but they are also navigating complex demands. When they have space to reflect with others and learn from shared experiences and best practices, they are better able to guide their teams and sustain a positive culture.
A similar shift shows up in how evidence is used. Applying research in real time is rarely straightforward, especially in settings where the pace is constant and priorities compete. When leaders can see how others are adapting the same practices in similar environments, the work becomes more accessible. It moves from something that feels theoretical to something that can be realistically integrated into daily care.
The same pattern extends to trust. Confidence in long-term care is built gradually through consistency and transparency. That kind of openness is difficult to maintain when leaders feel exposed or isolated. It becomes more sustainable when improvement is approached collectively, where data is discussed openly, and progress is understood as ongoing rather than perfect.
One of the most important lessons from this work is that collaboration cannot stop with long-term care organizations alone. The strongest progress happens when the work extends outward. When nursing homes engage with academic partners, gerontological centers of excellence, geriatric workforce programs, public health teams, emergency preparedness networks, ombudsman programs, quality improvement organizations, state agencies and senior living associations, the perspective widens.
The work becomes more connected to the larger system that supports older adults. These relationships bring added expertise, stronger alignment, and a shared sense of responsibility that benefits residents, families, and staff.
Leadership and learning
Taken together, these ideas reflect an evolution in leadership itself. The expectation is no longer that leaders hold all knowledge or solutions within their own buildings. Instead, leadership is becoming more connected, more adaptive and more grounded in shared learning.
That evolution is reflected in three qualities that continue to surface throughout this series. Strong leaders stay connected to others who understand the work. They remain curious, asking questions that uncover root causes and better approaches. And they show the kind of steady courage that allows them to be transparent, to adjust course, and keep moving forward.
Coalitions help make this possible. They provide a structured way to learn together, to exchange ideas, and to approach improvement as a shared effort. They also create a sense of steadiness in a field that is often under pressure, allowing leaders to step back, reflect and move forward with greater confidence.
We are seeing this take shape in Iowa, where coalition work is bringing organizations and partners together around common goals. Leaders are aligning their efforts, learning from one another, and strengthening outcomes across settings. What once depended on individual effort is becoming a collective process.
This is where long-term care leadership is heading. It is less about working independently and more about building strong connections that support meaningful, sustained improvement.
The future does not depend on leaders doing more on their own. It depends on leaders who are willing to stay connected, continue learning and lead with openness. The future of long-term care leadership is shared. And it is already underway.
Kim Bergen Jackson is an Associate Professor at the University of Iowa College of Nursing, a Board Certified Gerontological Nurse, and a Licensed Nursing Home Administrator. She brings deep expertise and a healthy dose of personality to her work advancing long-term care quality across Iowa. As a leader in the Iowa Nursing Home Quality Coalition, she helps drive statewide improvement through shared learning, community partnerships, and innovative approaches to care.
The opinions expressed in McKnight’s Long-Term Care News guest submissions are the author’s and are not necessarily those of McKnight’s Long-Term Care News or its editors.
Have a column idea? See our submission guidelines here.
summary: Progress in long-term care becomes more achievable when it is shared. Strong leaders stay connected to others who understand the work. This is where long-term care leadership is heading. The future of long-term care leadership is shared.

title: Avamere Exiting Skilled Nursing Business After 26-Property Sabra REIT Transition
description: Senior housing operator Avamere is getting out of the skilled nursing business. Avamere, which previously spun off its senior living holdings into senior living operator Arete Living in 2022, is transitioning out of management agreements for 26 properties owned by Sabra Healthcare REIT (Nasdaq: SBRA). Sabra is reassigning 22 of the properties to Cascadia Healthcare […] The post Avamere Exiting Skilled Nursing Business After 26-Property Sabra REIT Transition appeared first on Senior Housing News
content:
Senior housing operator Avamere is getting out of the skilled nursing business.
Avamere, which previously spun off its senior living holdings into senior living operator Arete Living in 2022, is transitioning out of management agreements for 26 properties owned by Sabra Healthcare REIT (Nasdaq: SBRA).
Sabra is reassigning 22 of the properties to Cascadia Healthcare and the other four to subsidiaries of an existing tenant that is a “national leader” in skilled nursing. With the transaction, Sabra is setting Cascadia’s closing rent at $53 million. The transaction is slated to close this year.
“The Avamere transition has been well-planned and is benefiting from a high level of cooperation across the various parties involved, following outreach to Sabra earlier this year by founder, Rick Miller, who indicated a desire to exit the skilled nursing business,” Sabra CEO Rick Matros said in a press release about the move.
Avamere has 57 locations listed on its website, including Ovation properties managed by its former spinoff, Arete Living, and it’s not clear how the operators will move forward with the change at Sabra.
A representative for Avamere didn’t immediately respond to a request for more information Tuesday.
Avamere spun off Arete Living in 2022 with intentions for the platform to serve as its dedicated senior living arm. The spinoff started under the leadership of President Sarah Spreitler-Silva with 28 senior living communities across seven states, including the Ovation brand that Avamere built in 2018 and the years to come.
The company launched Ovation in an attempt to create an “active lifestyle village” for people of different ages and acuities. The brand combined lifestyle-oriented units like independent living and villas and a technology suite supporting residents. Notably, the original concept eschewed skilled nursing entirely despite existing as a micro version of a CCRC.
As of early 2025, Arete Living managed 31 properties across six states, following a five-community acquisition. Today, Arete Living is listed as the brand name on multiple properties, and the company has posted numerous job openings for Arete communities in states like Oregon and New Mexico throughout the month of July.

title: Future Leader: Madisen Medley, Vice President of Business Development, Merrill Gardens
description: Madisen Medley, Vice President of Business Development at Merrill Gardens, has been named to the Future Leaders Class of 2026 by Senior Housing News. To become a Future Leader, an individual is nominated by their peers. The candidate must be a high-performing employee who is 40 years of age or younger, a passionate worker who […] The post Future Leader: Madisen Medley, Vice President of Business Development, Merrill Gardens appeared first on Senior Housing News .
content:
Madisen Medley, Vice President of Business Development at Merrill Gardens, has been named to the Future Leaders Class of 2026 by Senior Housing News.
To become a Future Leader, an individual is nominated by their peers. The candidate must be a high-performing employee who is 40 years of age or younger, a passionate worker who knows how to put vision into action, and an advocate for older adults and the committed professionals who ensure their well-being.
Medley sat down with Senior Housing News to share what drew her to the senior housing & senior living industry, the biggest leadership lessons she has learned, her thoughts on the future of senior housing & senior living, and much more. To learn more about the Future Leaders Awards program, visit https://futureleaders.wtwhmedia.com/.
SHN: What drew you to the senior housing & senior living industry?
Medley: My first real introduction to senior housing happened in high school through a volunteer program called Social Involvement Through Education and Service (SITES). Twice a week, I volunteered at an adult day care that specialized in dementia care. At 17, it was honestly intimidating. I was working with people who didn’t know who I was from one visit to the next. My grandparents were in great health, so I had very little frame of reference for what I was walking into.
But I have such vivid memories from my time with the residents. One day, someone put on a record, old music playing in the room, and a resident, who moments before had seemed so distant, was suddenly present. This woman was singing along, word for word. I remember going over to her and asking her, “Do you know this song?” She looked at me and said, “Honey, everybody knows this song.”
It sounds simple, but that moment has stayed with me. The connection between music and memory, between something I experience every day and a disease I barely understood. I’ve talked about it in graduate school applications as well as industry conversations. And honestly, it still makes me emotional.
From there, I went all in. I graduated with a double major in long-term care and healthcare administration, a minor in business, and a certificate in gerontology. I did multiple internships in rural geriatric services and SNFs, and I kept coming back to this space. After college, I worked briefly at the Cleveland Clinic and realized quickly that I missed the direct line to the people being served.
A neighbor of mine knew someone in senior housing based in Seattle, a gentleman overseeing the international division of Leisure Care, which was launching a new division and had an analyst role open. I interviewed, they took a chance on me, I moved across the country, and started a career I’ve never looked back on.
What surprised me most was discovering the private pay senior living world. Everything I had studied was rooted in the public side: skilled nursing, state regulations, Medicare, and Medicaid. Private pay senior living wasn’t even a concept that came up in my formal education. And what I found was a world where people opted in. They were choosing a lifestyle of engagement, community, and connection. Not a place they were resigned to, but a place they wanted to be. That distinction, between a place where you go to decline and a place where you go to thrive, is at the heart of what I believe our industry is really about. And it’s one of the reasons why I’ve stayed.
SHN: How would you describe your leadership style, and how has it changed over time?
Medley: If you had asked me this question earlier in my career, I’m not sure I would have had a very good answer.
When I started out, I had just graduated and was trying to find my footing in an industry I had studied but didn’t yet fully understand. I was a hard worker, that’s always been true, but my thinking was more singular. I believed there was usually one right way to approach a problem, and I leaned into that pretty hard. What I’ve learned over time is that the most important skill in this industry is learning how to hold multiple perspectives at once and find the path forward through them, not around them.
I recently asked a colleague how she would describe my leadership style, because I think it’s always more honest coming from someone who works with you than from yourself. She said: direct, adaptable, hands-on but not a micromanager, and very aware, meaning I stay connected across different parts of an organization rather than operating in a silo. That resonated with me.
I do take a 360-degree view. I try to understand a problem or opportunity from every angle before I move, and my role demands that, because on any given day I might be working with C-suite executives, general managers, sales directors, and construction teams. That range is actually my favorite thing about what I do.
The deeper evolution, though, has been about learning from the leaders around me. Over the course of my career, there have been people I wanted to grow up and become, people I’ve watched closely and learned from by observation, and people who have coached me directly. You take the pieces that resonate with you and build your own approach from there. I’m still building.
SHN: What is the biggest leadership lesson you’ve learned while serving the senior housing & senior living industry?
Medley: Professional vulnerability.
It sounds counterintuitive, especially as you advance in your career and people start to look to you as an expert, but I’ve come to believe it’s one of the most important skills a leader can develop, and this industry, maybe more than any other, has taught me that.
Senior housing sits at the intersection of real estate, healthcare, operations, hospitality, capital markets, policy, and technology. No one is a complete expert across all of it. And the way our industry is structured, with owners, operators, investors, lenders, nonprofits, and vendors all at the table, we have to work symbiotically. The best outcomes don’t come from the person in the room who has all the answers. They come from the person who knows how to ask the right questions and bring the right people together.
What I’ve learned is how to be in a room where I’m still learning something, not by pretending to know more than I do, but by coming in with a foundation, asking questions with intention, and staying genuinely open. That’s a skill I first started developing in graduate school, where you’re constantly put in situations outside your area of expertise and expected to contribute anyway. It prepared me well.
Being professionally vulnerable doesn’t mean being unprepared. It means being honest about where your edge is and having the confidence to operate right at that edge anyway.
SHN: In one word, how would you describe the senior housing & senior living industry and why?
Medley: Energizing.
I was actually asked a version of this question recently, but with three words instead of one. I chose ever-changing, energizing, and intuition. But if I have to pick just one, it’s energizing, because it captures both the industry and my relationship with it.
I chose this career early and deliberately, and after more than a decade, I’m still motivated by it every single day. Part of that comes from the people I work with across ownership groups, my company, operators, lenders, and vendors. There’s a genuine drive in this industry to get it right. Part of it comes from the scale of what’s ahead: the demographic tailwinds from the baby boomer generation represent one of the most significant opportunities in senior housing, and I feel fortunate to be building my career in the middle of that moment.
But honestly, the thing that energizes me most is the residents themselves. Anytime I get back into a community, which is never as often as I’d like given the nature of my role, it reminds me of why all of it matters. Every time I’m with a resident, I learn something new about how they engage with the environment we’re building for them, the programs we’re creating, and the community we’re trying to foster. That never gets old. And it keeps me honest about what we’re actually here to do.
SHN: What do you see as the biggest opportunities and challenges currently facing the senior housing & senior living industry?
Medley: I’d say the biggest challenge and the biggest opportunity are actually the same thing: serving the future resident.
On the challenge side, cost is real and it’s pressing, not just for residents and their families, but for operators trying to run sustainable businesses. As Medicaid and Social Security face continued pressure, and as the cost of staffing and delivering high-quality care continues to rise, we know that the people who need senior housing most may be the ones least able to afford it. And in private pay senior living, while we’re very much in a hospitality-oriented space, we are also care providers. The expectation for high-touch, high-quality care is only going to grow. Balancing that with financial sustainability is one of the defining challenges of our time.
But looking further out, beyond the tailwinds of the baby boomer generation, which are real and significant, I find myself asking what comes next. The demographic wave will crest. What does our industry look like on the other side of it? That’s a question I’m starting to think about more seriously, and it shapes how I think about what we’re building today.
The opportunity, though, is just as significant. The future resident is still figuring out what they want. This is a generation that has Amazon Prime delivering in three hours, with AI at their fingertips, and with more access to information and convenience than any generation before them. Will they want technology woven into every part of their living experience? Or will they crave something simpler?
My instinct, informed by watching what happened during COVID, when people moved to be closer to nature and closer to family and craved the human connection that was suddenly taken away, is that the future resident is going to want community above all else. The pandemic reminded us that you don’t know what you have until it’s gone.
So the opportunity for our industry is to be ready for that. Not just with the right physical environments, but with the right programming, the right staffing models, and the right approach to meeting people where they are, whether that’s in a senior living community or in the home they’re choosing to stay in. Technology may be how we deliver it, but human connection will be what we deliver.
SHN: If you had a crystal ball, what do you think will impact the senior housing & senior living industry over the next 5-10 years?
Medley: I think the most honest and obvious answer is that the decisions we’re making today will shape the next decade more than anything else.
Right now, we’re putting shovels in the ground on projects with three-year construction timelines. We’re building operational platforms and capital structures for a resident profile that is still evolving. That requires a kind of disciplined flexibility that our industry doesn’t always talk about, building for what we know is coming while staying adaptive enough to respond to what we can’t fully predict yet.
The baby boomer wave is undeniable. Virtually every major decision in senior housing right now, how we build, how we staff, what technology we invest in, and what programming looks like, is shaped by that demographic reality. And the tailwinds are real. But I’m also starting to think about what happens after the peak. Will we face oversupply? What does the cohort behind the boomers look like, and will they want the same things? These are questions worth asking.
On the operator side, I think about sustainability a lot. Capital is entering our space at a pace we haven’t seen before, which is exciting, but operators are in shorter supply. The ones who are successful know why: they stick with what they know and do it exceptionally well. As the customer evolves, operators will have to evolve too, and that balance between staying true to your core competency and adapting to a new customer is going to be a tension point.
What gives me the most hope is the level of collaboration I’m seeing across stakeholder groups, including owners, operators, lenders, vendors, nonprofits, and policymakers. More than ever before, there are people across every part of this ecosystem genuinely trying to solve the hard problems together.
SHN: In your opinion, what qualities must all Future Leaders possess?
Medley: First, a commitment to continuous learning, and I mean that in the broadest possible sense. I mean a genuine posture of curiosity that you carry into every room you enter.
This industry rewards people who keep asking questions, who seek out perspectives outside their own expertise, and who are willing to step into unfamiliar territory and do the work. I’ve seen this be the differentiating quality in the leaders I most admire. The ones who are still growing, still learning, and still curious are the ones driving this industry forward.
The second quality is a sense of levity. The ability to stay solution-focused when things go wrong, to not take yourself so seriously that you can’t adapt, and to bring some lightness to a room that keeps people moving forward.
In an industry that deals with some of the most real human experiences, aging, memory loss, and the end of life, the leaders who can hold both the hardship and the humor of our work are the ones people want to follow. They’re the ones who build teams that last.

title: Staff retention changes everything
description: My mother’s friend is a dear lady who lives in an assisted living (AL) community and has way too much experience with hospitals and SNFs. She is an expert on a perspective that we rarely consider. Her Medicaid waiver AL was recently sold, and the new owners are cutting back on perks that likely drove…
content:
My mother’s friend is a dear lady who lives in an assisted living (AL) community and has way too much experience with hospitals and SNFs. She is an expert on a perspective that we rarely consider.
Her Medicaid waiver AL was recently sold, and the new owners are cutting back on perks that likely drove the previous owners to sell. I don’t have to tell you how difficult it can be to survive in a Medicaid world.
Her major complaint is staff turnover. Since the sale, most of the staff have moved on, and the new staff haven’t provided the care she’s used to. I don’t have to tell you how difficult that can be either. I also don’t have to tell you how unhappy she is.
Over the last several years, our SNFs, hospitals and ALs have seen a flood of skilled front-line staff pour out the door. Many of those people have left healthcare altogether. Can we blame the pandemic? Maybe.
Many of my own staff, those with speech therapy and occupational therapy Master’s degrees and physical therapy doctorates, left five years ago to become real estate agents, medical text copy editors and business owners. All that education and expertise gone, just to get away from the stress and unexpected and unimaginable demands of very-real life-or-death situations.
My daughter-in-law is the associate dean of health at a local university, and she says they aren’t getting nursing applicants; what used to be the go-to degree for science-minded men and women has empty seats.
The Baltimore Sun recently exposed the problem of fatigue among nurses, but the extent reaches farther across the healthcare continuum. Amid ongoing burnout, staffing shortages, and growing complexity across the healthcare continuum, nurses, nursing assistants and therapists have never played a more essential role.
Protecting staff that go above and beyond
The need is beyond the bedside. The best clinicians see the whole picture. They’re not just focused on giving the meds at the right time; they’re helping families understand a diagnosis. They’re identifying barriers to care before they become crises. The best of us understand people, systems, and solutions and help navigate between them.
The demand for healthcare continues to grow faster than the systems that are supposed to train and support the workers, because the communities in need continue to grow. And these healthcare workers are not just needed in the traditional roles of hospital, SNF or home care.
We see clinicians branching into public health, policy, research, maternal health and health equity. The best nurse wants to see a healthy population no matter where they work, and person-centered care is always the focus.
Supporting our healthcare workers in our SNFs has to be a priority in all aspects of our management. It’s not just pizza during Nursing Home Week.
Open your doors for your staff to seek support when they’re feeling burned out. Treat resilience and self-care as professional competency, not afterthoughts.
Teach your staff how to recognize burnout in themselves and their colleagues and practice boundary-setting and reflective techniques that carry into their careers. Offer counseling services, peer support groups and staff trained to notice when someone is struggling. Recognize that healthcare professional burnout requires sustainable policy solutions, not just coping strategies.
Healthcare is one of the few professions that is unlikely to be replaced by technology. It’s one of the few professions that can directly change lives, even in the face of challenging and shifting realities. We are grounded in science, driven by purpose, and profoundly essential to the sustainability of our population. We need to keep our best and brightest supported, happy and retained.
Jean Wendland Porter, PT, CCI, WCC, CKTP, CDP, TWD, is the regional director of therapy operations at Diversified Health Partners in Ohio.
